name.text: Application of scale factors to a cylindrical model

questiontext.text:
An engineering firm has designed a prototype for a chemical storage tank. The prototype consists of a cylindrical body and a hemispherical top. The dimensions of the prototype are: {@r_p@} m Radius and a Cylindrical Height of {@h_p@} m

The firm decides to produce an industrial version of the tank by enlarging every dimension by a linear scale factor of {@k@}.

[[jsxgraph width="350px" height="400px"]] var board = JXG.JSXGraph.initBoard(divid, { boundingbox: [-10, 20, 10, -5], axis: false, showCopyright: false }); var R = {#r_p#} * 1.5; var H = {#h_p#} * 1.5; var p = 0.3; /* ===================== HEMISPHERICAL TOP ===================== */ // top visible arc var hemiTop = board.create('curve', [ function(t){return R*Math.cos(t)}, function(t){return H + R*Math.sin(t)}, 0, Math.PI ], { strokeColor: '#1f77b4', strokeWidth: 3 }); // bottom hidden arc var hemiBottom = board.create('curve', [ function(t){return R*Math.cos(t)}, function(t){return H + (R*p)*Math.sin(t)}, Math.PI, 2*Math.PI ], { strokeColor: '#1f77b4', dash: 2, strokeWidth: 2 }); /* ===================== CYLINDER SIDES ===================== */ var leftSide = board.create('segment', [[-R,0],[-R,H]], { strokeColor:'#2ca02c', strokeWidth:3 }); var rightSide = board.create('segment', [[R,0],[R,H]], { strokeColor:'#2ca02c', strokeWidth:3 }); /* ===================== CYLINDER BASE ===================== */ // visible base board.create('curve', [ function(t){return R*Math.cos(t)}, function(t){return (R*p)*Math.sin(t)}, Math.PI,2*Math.PI ], { strokeColor:'#2ca02c', strokeWidth:3 }); // hidden base board.create('curve', [ function(t){return R*Math.cos(t)}, function(t){return (R*p)*Math.sin(t)}, 0,Math.PI ], { strokeColor:'#2ca02c', dash:2, strokeWidth:2 }); /* ===================== HEIGHT LABEL ===================== */ var heightLine = board.create('segment', [[R+1,0],[R+1,H]], {strokeWidth:2, strokeColor:'black', dash:1}); board.create('text', [R+1.5, H/2, "Height = {#h_p#} m"]); /* ===================== RADIUS LABEL ===================== */ var radiusLine = board.create('segment', [[0,H],[R,H]], {strokeWidth:2, strokeColor:'black'}); board.create('text', [R/2, H+1, "Radius = {#r_p#} m"]); /* ===================== TITLE LABEL ===================== */ board.create('text',[-4, H+R+2, "Prototype Fountain"]); [[/jsxgraph]]

Complete the following regarding the enlargement:

  1. State the Linear Scale Factor \(k\): [[input:ans1]] [[validation:ans1]] [[feedback:prt1]]
  2. Calculate the Area Scale Factor \(k^2\): [[input:ans2]][[validation:ans2]] [[feedback:prt2]]
  3. Calculate the Volume Scale Factor \(k^3\): [[input:ans3]] [[validation:ans3]] [[feedback:prt3]]
  4. If the prototype has a volume of \({@vol_p@}\) m³, calculate the volume of the industrial tank: [[input:ans4]] [[validation:ans4]] [[feedback:prt4]] m³

( Round your final volume to 2 decimal places.)

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

To solve problems involving similarity and enlargement, we apply the scale factor relationship across dimensions:

1. Linear Scale Factor \(k\)

\(\begin{align} k &= \frac{\text{Image length}}{\text{Object length}} \\ k &= {@ta1@} \end{align}\)

2. Area Scale Factor \(k^2\)

The area of the enlarged image increases by the square of the linear scale factor:

\(\begin{align} \text{Area Scale Factor} &= k^2 \\ &= {@ta1@}^2 \\ &= {@ta2@} \end{align}\)

3. Volume Scale Factor \(k^3\)

The volume of the enlarged image increases by the cube of the linear scale factor:

\(\begin{align} \text{Volume Scale Factor} &= k^3 \\ &= {@ta1@}^3 \\ &= {@ta3@} \end{align}\)

4. Capacity of the Industrial Tank

\(\begin{align} V_{\text{image}} &= V_{\text{object}} \times k^3 \\ &= {@float(vol_p)@} \times {@ta3@} \\ &= {@ta4@} \text{ m}^3 \end{align}\)

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done.

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.
